request.question: 2002 animated film Groove Squad was one of many made-for-TV movies, such as Inspector Gadget and Dennis the Menace, on what series?

title: Inspector Gadget (film)
Inspector Gadget (1983 TV series)
Inspector Gadget: Mission 1 – Global Terror!
Inspector Gadget's Field Trip
Groove Squad
Inspector Gadget Saves Christmas
Gadget & the Gadgetinis
Inspector Gadget 2
Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons
Inspector Gadget
sentences: Inspector Gadget is a 1999 American action-comedy film directed by David Kellogg, written by Kerry Ehrin and Zak Penn from a story by Ehrin and Dana Olsen.
It is loosely based on the 1983 animated cartoon series of the same name.
It stars Matthew Broderick as the title character, along with Rupert Everett as Dr. Claw, Michelle Trachtenberg as Penny, and Dabney Coleman as Chief Quimby.
Three new characters were introduced, Dr. Brenda Bradford (played by Joely Fisher), Mayor Wilson (played by Cheri Oteri) and the Gadgetmobile (voiced by D. L. Hughley).
The film tells the story of how Inspector Gadget and Dr. Claw came to be in the cartoon.

Inspector Gadget is a French-Canadian-American animated comic science fiction television series that revolves around the adventures of a clumsy, dim-witted detective named Inspector Gadget—a human being with various bionic gadgets built into his body.
Gadget's nemesis is Dr. Claw, the leader of an evil organization known as "M.A.D." The series launched the "Inspector Gadget" franchise.
Inspector Gadget: Mission 1 – Global Terror!
is a graphic adventure game based on the animated television series "Inspector Gadget" and released for DOS in 1992.
In the game, Gadget is assigned a mission to rescue UN members who have been kidnapped and scattered around the world by his nemesis Dr. Claw.
Unlike other "Inspector Gadget" games, players do not have direct control over Gadget himself, but rather his niece Penny and their dog Brain, who must assist the clueless Gadget in his mission.

Inspector Gadget's Field Trip (onscreen title: Field Trip Starring Inspector Gadget) is a spin-off incarnation of Inspector Gadget, produced by DIC Entertainment in 1996 and aired on the The History Channel, where it was rerun until 2000.
26 episodes mixing animation with live-action were produced.
Don Adams returned as the voice of Inspector Gadget.

The series was an educational travelogue program for children, in which the animated Gadget would show viewers the many different sites in famous cities around the world via live-action-clips with historical facts.
The theme song is slightly similar to the one in the Gadget Boy series.
In fact, Gadget Boy sometimes makes a cameo appearance in the show (e.g. St. Augustine, Florida episode).
Groove Squad is a 2002 animated film which debuted as a part of Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons in late 2002.
The squad consists of three hip cheerleaders and the team mascot.
The Cheerleaders are accidentally turned into superheroes by a deranged genius.
They must defeat the power-hungry mad scientist while struggling to win the annual cheerleading competition.
Inspector Gadget Saves Christmas is a Christmas television special, featuring characters from the cartoon series "Inspector Gadget".
The special was produced by DIC Entertainment and LBS Communications, Inc., and aired on NBC on December 4, 1992.

Gadget & the Gadgetinis is a Canadian–French-Italian TV series spin-off of the 1983 series "Inspector Gadget", developed by DiC Entertainment in cooperation with Haim Saban's SIP Animation and released 2001–2003.
There are 52 episodes.
"Gadget & the Gadgetinis" is today the property of DHX Media, which holds the rights to most of DiC's library.

Inspector Gadget 2 (alternatively known as IG2, and credited as just Inspector 2 Gadget at the start credits) is a 2003 Australian-American live-action comedy film, released direct-to-video on VHS and DVD on March 11, 2003.
The film is based on the cartoon series created by DiC Entertainment and is a stand-alone sequel to the 1999 film "Inspector Gadget".
It was filmed in Brisbane, Queensland, Australia.
Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons was a series of animated made-for-TV movies, which lasted for just one season (2002) on Nickelodeon.
It started on October 6, 2002 and ended on December 29, 2002.

Produced by DIC Entertainment, the made-for-TV films are either based on various DIC productions such as "Inspector Gadget", "", and "Dennis the Menace", or are loose adaptations of stories from classic literature such as "Treasure Island" and "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea".
One film, "Groove Squad", which was about a group of crimefighting cheerleaders, was an original story.
All of these films were later released to VHS and DVD in the U.S. by MGM Home Entertainment under the "DIC Movie Toons" label.
In 2008, Gaiam re-released the films on DVD.
Inspector Gadget is a media franchise that began in 1983 with the DIC Entertainment animated television series, "Inspector Gadget".
Since the original series, there have been many spin-offs based on the show, including additional animated series, video games and films.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

It started on October 6, 2002 and ended on December 29, 2002.  Produced by DIC Entertainment, the made-for-TV films are either based on various DIC productions such as "Inspector Gadget", "", and "Dennis the Menace", or are loose adaptations of stories from classic literature such as "Treasure Island" and "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea".  One film, "Groove Squad", which was about a group of crimefighting cheerleaders, was an original story.  All of these films were later released to VHS and DVD in the U.S. by MGM Home Entertainment under the "DIC Movie Toons" label.  In 2008, Gaiam re-released the films on DVD.

Groove Squad | Groove Squad is a 2002 animated film which debuted as a part of Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons in late 2002.  The squad consists of three hip cheerleaders and the team mascot.  The Cheerleaders are accidentally turned into superheroes by a deranged genius.  They must defeat the power-hungry mad scientist while struggling to win the annual cheerleading competition.

Sabrina: Friends Forever | Sabrina: Friends Forever (also known as Sabrina the Teenage Witch in Friends Forever) is a 2002 animated television film produced by DIC Entertainment and was first shown on "Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons" in the United States (however, in most other countries, it premiered on either Disney Channel or Toon Disney).  It is a continuation of the animated television series "" and was followed by another television series, "Sabrina's Secret Life".

Ned's Declassified School Survival Guide | Ned's Declassified School Survival Guide (sometimes shortened to "Ned's Declassified") is an American live action sitcom on Nickelodeon that debuted in the Nickelodeon Sunday night TEENick scheduling block on September 12, 2004.  The series' original pilot episode aired on September 7, 2003, without many of the current version's main characters, and it was ordered to series by Nickelodeon in early 2004.  The one-hour special movie series finale aired on June 8, 2007.

Mary-Kate and Ashley in Action! | Mary-Kate and Ashley in Action!  is an animated television series featuring the voices and likeness of Mary-Kate and Ashley Olsen.  It is also a series of books that spun off, from the show.  The show premiered on October 20, 2001 on the ABC block Disney's One Saturday Morning, and was cancelled after one season due to negative ratings.  Reruns were later shown on Toon Disney and will be seen on Nickelodeon in 2016, as part of the network's purchasing of the television rights to Mary-Kate and Ashley's TV series and movies.

Toon City | Toon City is an animation studio located in Manila, Philippines.  Its primary contractor is The Walt Disney Company and its DisneyToon Studios division, which produces animated TV series and direct-to-video films.  They have also done a few commercials and several direct-to-video work for Nickelodeon, Universal, Warner Bros. and Cinegroupe.
